Data.MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as this distressed and troubled person. Taste the taste of sap and bark in your mouth as you nom on the tree with all your might.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture a very distressed condor watching you munch your mouth all over its tree house. You've made a condor enemy this day.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You thought you were the most <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> person in this situation, but then you look up and see a very equally distressed and troubled <reading>con</reading>dor (こん). The tree you've been mouthing is its home. And now you've slobbered all over it, ruining this condor's day.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you put your <radical>mouth</radical> on a <radical>tree</radical> and try to eat it, you’re probably a <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> individual (unless you’re a woodchuck). You don’t need to eat this tree, you have plenty of food at home. Get your mouth off that tree you distressed child.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a disaster so bad that there are floods AND fires. That's too much!
Data.ReadingHint: You better figure out a way to stop your cyborg from causing more disasters like this. Fix it now, or you're gonna be in big trouble.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>disaster</kanji> was caused by your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)! It's malfunctioning, running around leaking large amounts of water and breathing fire. I guess you didn't work out all the kinks before you let it roam wild.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>flood</radical> and a <radical>fire</radical>! At the same time? This is a <kanji>disaster</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine this drunkard, squatting down under the tree to poop. You can smell the poop and a faint stench of alcohol in the air. How could he not have realized that there was a machine toilet right there?
Data.ReadingHint: Listen to the drunkard grumbling about the lack of a key as he poops under the tree. He doesn't sound happy, and every time he stresses to push out the poop, his voice fluctuates. Imagine hearing this in your mind and being a wee bit disgusted.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Oh, I see — the door to the outhouse with the <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet is locked, and the drunkard doesn't have the <reading>key</reading> (き). If he had a machine like a jackhammer, or a circular saw, or some other heavy duty machine, he might be able to get in without a key, but since he has none of those or a key, he's reduced to pooping under the tree instead.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Next to a <radical>tree</radical>, you spot <radical>poop</radical>, not once but twice. Only a <radical>drunkard</radical> would be the type of <radical>person</radical> to not use the nearby <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet. There's a perfectly good outhouse with a machine toilet right there, but this drunkard is so drunk that he decides to poop under the tree instead. And not only does he poop once, he poops twice.

Data.MeaningHint: You take a peek under the lid to check, and your poop explosion is one hundred percent gone! Phew!
Data.ReadingHint: Look around you at the classy surroundings of the Ritz Hotel bathroom. You feel one hundred percent relieved now, and ready to enjoy your date at the Ritz!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: It was extra important that there was zero <kanji>percent</kanji> poop left, because you're not on a date just anywhere, you're at the <reading>Ritz</reading> (りつ)!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Sitting on the <radical>lid</radical> of a toilet, you're praying your <radical>poop</radical> <radical>explosion</radical> will be flushed away, beseeching the <radical>cross</radical> decorating the bathroom wall. The poop needs to be one hundred <kanji>percent</kanji> flushed away because you're on a date, and you don't want even one percent of your explosion left for them to see.

Data.ReadingHint: You’re flying through the air super fast. Feel the air rushing past you as heat builds up all over your body from rocketing through the air so fast.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When you <kanji>fly</kanji>, you generate a lot of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) because you’re moving so fast through the atmosphere. Normally this heat would be a nuisance, but you’re a superhero so it doesn’t bother you. This is actually the reason you’re able to fly – you’re a superhero and super resistant to heat.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>fly</radical> radical and the <kanji>fly</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Awesome!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine that moment as you fall off the roof. In slow motion you can see the roof moving away, you see yourself as a little kid, a teen, an adult, then BAM you've injured your mouth. Ow ow ow!
Data.ReadingHint: No matter how much you try to wave your hands and gesture, they keep calling you Guy. It's really annoying, actually, because you aren't a guy and you aren't Guy either. But your mouth is too damaged for you to tell him.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you try to assess the <kanji>damage</kanji>, you hear someone shout, "<reading>Guy</reading> (がい), hey, you okay, guy?" They run up to you, but your mouth is too <kanji>damage</kanji>d, you can't respond. "Okay guy, I'm gonna call you Guy now. Let's get you to the hospital."
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You slip off the <radical>roof</radical>, your <radical>life</radical> flashes before your eyes, and you smash your <radical>mouth</radical> on the ground causing some serious <kanji>damage</kanji>. Oof, this is a really bad <kanji>injury</kanji>, you fell all the way off the roof and onto your mouth.

Data.MeaningHint: The hat you’re wearing is ridiculous, giving you a surplus of bad style. Imagine a bunch of people scowling at you, not only because of your crazy hat, but because of the annoying sound of the jackhammer. Hear the obnoxious noise around you as the jackhammer rattles your bones.
Data.ReadingHint: Your hat is doing little to defend you from the onslaught of yo-yos being tossed at you. Feel the yo-yos hitting your body and listen to them roll away after striking you.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: One of the people walking by is the owner of a push cart <reading>yo</reading>-yo (よ) stand. Having a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of yo-yos, he starts throwing them at you. This makes you stop using the jackhammer as you need both hands to fend off the yo-yo barrage.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Regardless of whether or not you wear a <radical>hat</radical> while you <radical>jackhammer</radical>, you’re going to have a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of noise. Jackhammers are loud, and your hat is doing nothing to dampen the racket. There’s a surplus of commotion coming from the jackhammer and onlookers are not amused.

Data.MeaningHint: Try and picture a Chinese turkey in your brain. Now imagine yourself trying to catch it, but you just can’t. It’s too difficult. The Chinese turkey is too elusive.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself entering a bakery, frantically scouring through all the Naan in an attempt to find one that’s actually a Chinese turkey. "WHERE ARE THE TURKEYS?", you scream, to everyone else’s bewilderment. Those elusive, difficult to find Chinese Naan turkeys…
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Do you know the actual reason Chinese turkey are so <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find? It’s because they’re all made out of <reading>Naan</reading> (なん). Of course they’re elusive when they blend in so well with all the other Naan!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Chinese</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find because they are so rare. Chinese turkeys are the rarest variety of turkey, so naturally, finding one is exceedingly difficult.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the most uncomfortable outfit you can. Your body is incurring some minor damage right now, but hey — at least you're covering your skin properly.
Data.ReadingHint: Even if you incur some damage from uncomfortable clothing, it's better to cover up and retain heat than to underdress and incur damage from frostbite or something.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Another reason to <kanji>cover</kanji> yourself is to retain <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Actually, retaining heat and staying warm is probably the reason people started covering themselves up in the first place.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: That <radical>outfit</radical> on your <radical>skin</radical> is there to <kanji>cover</kanji> you up. In polite society, it's expected that you cover your skin with some kind of outfit — even if you have to wear something that causes you to <kanji>incur</kanji> damage, like circulation-cutting skinny jeans or a shirt with a really scratchy tag.

Data.MeaningHint: See the green mountains rising on either side of the valley, hear the birdsong in the distance… and behold the miles of clothes racks that hold all your outfits. This valley is the only space big enough to contain your material abundance and wealth.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture these wealthy youths as they peruse your valley of outfits. They're mostly influencers making short-form video content for their socials, basically leeching off your abundance. Ugh. Is this how people get wealthy in the 21st century?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This valley is also <kanji>abundant</kanji> with <kanji>wealthy</kanji> <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) — trendy, wealthy youths who come from around the world to descend on your valley and peep your outfit collection.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You have an <radical>outfit</radical> <radical>valley</radical>, which is a valley where you store your <kanji>abundant</kanji> number of outfits. Yes, you're so <kanji>wealthy</kanji> that not only can you purchase a valley for yourself, you can also afford to use it like a walk-in closet.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture this happening in real time. First it starts to rain really hard, then the ground starts to shift and run, then everything is shaking and quaking so violently it's an earthquake!
Data.ReadingHint: You grab your shin, but the earthquake shakes the coffee table toward you to hit your shin yet again! Ow ow ow stop!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>earthquake</kanji> shakes you so violently that you fall over, banging your <reading>shin</reading> (しん) on the coffee table. The edge of the coffee table is very sharp, so it cuts into your shin pretty badly.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> causes a <radical>landslide</radical> that causes an <kanji>earthquake</kanji>!!! The earth doesn't just slide, it <kanji>shake</kanji>s and <kanji>quake</kanji>s.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine taking these nine flags... count 'em... and putting them up your butt. With each one that's up there it gets harder and harder to fit the next one in.
Data.ReadingHint: In your head, doing the Siri voice, come up with an answer for your nine-flags-in-the-butt predicament.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Now that you have nine flags inside your <kanji>butt</kanji>, you have to get them out. But, you don't know how to. So you ask <reading>Siri</reading> (しり), "How do I get nine flags out of my butt?"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You take some <radical>flag</radical>s, <radical>nine</radical> in total, and put them one by one up your <kanji>butt</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: What kind of animal could it be? You start to feel the soft fur with your hand and then the stench of death hits your nostrils. Ew.
Data.ReadingHint: See the bees swarming out from around the tail and toward your face. Run! Run before there’s nothing left of you but your own tail!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You try to pull the flag out of the ground and hear a rumbling buzzing sound. At the end of the flagpole you see a huge <reading>bee</reading>hive (び)! These bees made their home under the <kanji>tail</kanji>! Maybe that’s why the flag was sticking in it… to warn people. Oops.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>flag</radical> stuck in the ground with a little bit of <radical>fur</radical> coming out from it and on closer inspection you realize it’s actually a <kanji>tail</kanji>. Someone stuck a flag into some sort of animal, pushing it under the ground. All that’s visible now is some fur and part of the tail.

Data.MeaningHint: Can you even imagine twenty completely unique contraptions? Why don't you try, just for fun.
Data.ReadingHint: "Kayak contraptions" is pretty vague, but you do have a kayak, so you're tempted anyway. If only you could figure out what each contraption did so you could justify buying some.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You want to know what these <kanji>contraptions</kanji> are for, so you ask. The drunkard says, "These are <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) contraptions *urp* they're for your uh *blarp* kayak... things."
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>tree</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> sits with <radical>twenty</radical> little <kanji>contraptions</kanji>. There's little electronic ones, little bopping ones, little walking ones... twenty of them.

Data.MeaningHint: Try hitting a sponge on a turkey's forehead. Not certain. Now a stone. <kanji>Certain</kanji>.
Data.ReadingHint: As you smash the stone into the turkey's forehead, yell out "This is for you, my cock! THIS IS FOR YOU!" Your cock lover watches from the shadows.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're killing this turkey, and you need to make sure he's dead for <kanji>certain</kanji>. The reason you need to make sure this turkey is dead is because of the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) in your life. Male chickens get really jealous when turkeys are around.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>stone</radical> to the <radical>forehead</radical> of a <radical>turkey</radical>. It is <kanji>certain</kanji> that you will damage it.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine yourself feeling this strong dislike towards the Mona Lisa. She gets all the attention and you get none. It’s not fair and you’re upset.
Data.ReadingHint: Ken walks slowly toward the Mona Lisa, dislike shines in his eyes, but then! He falls for her too. No! Ken, destroy her!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>dislike</kanji> the Mona Lisa so much you hire <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai to cut her up.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>woman</radical> sees the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>, she feels a strong feeling of <kanji>dislike</kanji>. All women dislike the Mona Lisa because of how much attention she gets.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine that leader's old mouth saying things that make them stand apart from the others. They're individual from everyone else.
Data.ReadingHint: "You must be your own individual. Don't be sheeple," he says to the 子s.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As an <kanji>individual</kanji>, they must teach all the <reading>子</reading>s (こ) their way. Their individual way.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> has grown <radical>old</radical> from saying so many things. This is what makes them different from everyone else, they speak truly like an <kanji>individual</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine being down there under that heavy cliff. Imagine feeling the cliff's pressure as you watch it compress the dirt. It's so hard to breathe... h... help!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine yelling out to the dirt to encourage it: "Ah, two cliffs is nothing! Come on, you got this!" You're the dirt's gym buddy, and you won't let it buckle under the pressure.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You want to increase the <kanji>pressure</kanji>, so you stack on a second cliff. "<reading>Ah, two</reading> (あつ)?!" the dirt cries out in dismay. This dirt is tough, but it's really starting to feel the pressure now.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Under any <radical>cliff</radical>, there's some <radical>dirt</radical> that's under a lot of <kanji>pressure</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine seeing a narwhal sticking a stick, and maybe even its horn too, into the dirt on land. It needs to know if life exists outside of the ocean for sure.
Data.ReadingHint: Hear the plinky plink of the xylophone and think about what exists in this world. It's a sad song, you realize.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The narwhal can't find anything but it can't stop thinking about what could <kanji>exist</kanji> outside of its world. It pulls out a <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい) and starts tapping the keys with its horn. It plays a sad song about the meaning of life and whether existence is worth it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>narwhal</radical> sticks a <radical>stick</radical> into the <radical>dirt</radical> to see what might <kanji>exist</kanji> underneath it. Narwhals live in the sea, you see. So they're convinced nothing can exist anywhere else.

Data.MeaningHint: Watch yourself sleeping. Then, a net of flowers appear on your forehead as you start to dream.
Data.ReadingHint: While still dreaming, you say "moo" out loud too. "Moooo!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>dream</kanji> about cows saying "<reading>moo</reading> (む), moo, moo!"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>flowers</radical> <radical>net</radical> is upon your <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>evening</radical>. These appear on your head at night whenever you are having a <kanji>dream</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: The baby is falling off the cliff! Imagine the sheer terror you feel as the baby plummets towards the ground below.
Data.ReadingHint: Oh Santa-san. You're always here for me when I need you most. You can keep all my property and give it away as presents.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>give birth</kanji>, and now this happens. Such bad luck. But wait! What's that sound? Sleigh bells? Could it be?! It's <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん)! He caught your baby! And all your <kanji>property</kanji>.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>stand</radical> on the <radical>Cliff</radical> of <radical>Life</radical> and <kanji>give birth</kanji> to a child. Oh no! You’re standing too close to the edge of the Cliff of Life! The baby falls off the cliff right after you give birth to it! In your despair, you throw all your <kanji>property</kanji> off the cliff too.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a mole sneaking up behind the leader mole and stabbing him with a knife. This type of overthrowing of leadership feels familiar to you. Maybe this has happened before in the past?
Data.ReadingHint: Picture this like an exaggerated reenactment on the History channel. Leader after leader of とうきょう gets overthrown and replaced with the next guy. It never stops. (Until the end of the Edo period, I guess.)
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Where do leaders get <kanji>overthrow</kanji>n the most? In <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Just think back in Japanese history, back when everyone was at war with one another. People were taking over and getting overthrown and then that guy gets overthrown and then THAT guy gets overthrown. Basically, it used to be hard to stay a leader in とうきょう.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Someone stabbed the <radical>leader</radical> <radical>mole</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> to <kanji>overthrow</kanji> it. Now there is a new mole leader. Because that's what happens when you overthrow someone—someone else needs to take over. In this case, a vengeful mole.

Data.MeaningHint: Seriously. If you or someone else is stinking to high heaven, embrace a big bar of self care.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine trying to use a big bar of soap on yourself to get the stinky 草 off. But it's not working... the 草 stink is SO STRONG!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're <kanji>stinking</kanji> up the room because you spent your day rolling in <reading>草</reading> (くさ). And based on this particular stench, I'd say an animal left something in that 草 too...
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Wash your<radical>self</radical> with a <radical>big</radical> bar of soap if you're <kanji>stinking</kanji> everything up.

Data.MeaningHint: I mean, this unibrow is really, really thick. So thick it doesn’t even look real. But it is. It’s really real and really thick.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine the sun noticing its mistake and apologizing to the child. "Ah, two thick unibrows wasn't what you wanted? Wow, I'm sorry. This is embarrassing… I'm actually not sure I can remove the second one now that it's on there."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The child glances up at their <kanji>thick</kanji> unibrow and yells, "<reading>ah, two</reading> (あつ)!" Yup, it turns out the sun misunderstood the child's pleas for help. Instead of pulling them up from the cliff, it gave them a second thick unibrow on top of the first one.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Hanging off the edge of a <radical>cliff</radical> is a <radical>sun</radical>-worshipping <radical>child</radical> with a <kanji>thick</kanji> unibrow. Hanging off the cliff, the child cries out to the sun to save them from this cruel fate. Even the thickness of their unibrow is not enough to give the child the strength to climb back up on their own.

Data.MeaningHint: Oh, how you miss your Wolverine woman and her long, sharp claws… Now all you've got left to remember her by is this cross. Your poor, poor wife.
Data.ReadingHint: All your wife needs is a little cyborg tune-up. Bionic brain here, some new claws there. Yep, your cy-wife's as good as new.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Wait a minute… She can't be dead because your <kanji>wife</kanji> is a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)! How could you forget?!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You place a <radical>cross</radical> on a <radical>Wolverine</radical> <radical>woman</radical>. She's gone now, but she was once your <kanji>wife</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself in a deliberation with some other people. They think what they're saying is right, and it drives you mad. Then you realize that you're doing the same thing.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine how the deliberation concludes. Then, at the end, someone says "This deliberation is over! We have decided to _________" (in regards to the guillotine).
Data.ReadingMnemonic: What are you in <kanji>deliberation</kanji> about? Whether or not to send some poor soul to the <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). "I think he should go," you say. "I don't think he should," says another. This <kanji>deliberation</kanji> is going nowhere...
Data.MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> is <radical>righteousness</radical>, at least that's what you believe. You keep talking and saying these things, and you don't listen to anyone else's words. This is your style of <kanji>deliberation</kanji> and <kanji>discussion</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a snake as a hardened criminal. See this snake with many prison tattoos and muscles from working out in his jail cell. Hear the snake hissing as it slithers about to commit yet another crime.
Data.ReadingHint: Now see Han Solo doing battle with the snake (cue Star Wars music). The snake hisses and lunges at Han, but Han shoots the snake and it’s incapacitated. Smell the smell of scorched snake flesh in your nose.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This snake has evaded the law for too long. It’s time for a true hero to put an end to its <kanji>crime</kanji> spree. Enter <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo. Han Solo is the only man strong and brave enough to put an end to this snake and his crime filled ways.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>animal</radical> kingdom, a <radical>snake</radical> is the most likely to commit a <kanji>crime</kanji>. Remember the Garden of Eden? The snake was the bad guy. Ever since then, snakes are the animal most known for committing crimes.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine throwing a net around someone you think, no, you know is guilty of doing something bad to you. It feels pretty nice.
Data.ReadingHint: Woah! The xylophone is huge, how did they... well whatever, you definitely have proof that they're guilty now and the cops should be here any minute to take your criminal catch.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>guilt</kanji> shows on the criminal's face. They smile, shrug, and pull a stolen <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい) out of their back pocket.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>net</radical> around a <radical>criminal</radical> to show their <kanji>guilt</kanji>. Criminals look like anyone else on the street, so you decide to tie them in nets to make sure people know who they're dealing with.

Data.MeaningHint: There's a building right there. Try to go that direction. Oh, did the building prevent you? Told you so.
Data.ReadingHint: Your bowling antics are now preventing a bunch of people from going the directions they want to go. Nice job.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The building is <kanji>prevent</kanji>ing you from going the right direction, so you pull a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball out of your bag, aim, and bowl the building over. The building, and all the ones around it, go tumbling down. But now the rubble is preventing you from going further.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If there is a <radical>building</radical> in the <radical>direction</radical> you want to go it will <kanji>prevent</kanji> you from going any further. Like, come on kid, you think you can just walk right through a building? Don't make me laugh!

Data.MeaningHint: This theory you've thought of is great, it's going to change everything!
Data.ReadingHint: "Ron? Ron, what's your theory? What's your theory, Ron?" Hermione asks over and over. But you freeze. You don't remember who you are. "Hermione, I have a theory that I was just a person learning Japanese, and not a magical boy at all." "Shut up Ron, that's a stupid theory," she replies.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: "What is your theory, <reading>Ron</reading> (ろん)?" asks Hermione. You're Ron Weasley, and you just came up with a theory that you think will help you stop Voldemort.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical>, throwing your <radical>hat</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> in front of the <radical>bookshelf</radical>, "I have a <kanji>theory</kanji>!" All this reading and thinking has finally given you a wonderful idea, a theory you think might solve all of your problems!

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself coming back to the same tombstone each passing year and tying a new thread to it. You're trying to manage these feelings by keeping track of how much time passes.
Data.ReadingHint: Maybe this is your future tombstone, and eating cake each year is a preemptive celebration of your death day! Ooh, what a spooky theory.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Dealing with the <kanji>passage of time</kanji> can be tough, so after you tie the thread to the tombstone, each year you eat a piece of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). You already eat cake to mark the passage of time on your birthday, so why not here as well?
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Tying a <radical>thread</radical> to a <radical>tombstone</radical> each year will help you <kanji>manage</kanji> the <kanji>passage of time</kanji>. That way you’ll know how many years you <kanji>pass through</kanji>, since you’ll have one thread for each year to count.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the pain and confusion of being stabbed by a violent tree. You hit your head on the tree again, as if to test it, and again a knife falls out and stabs you. Oh, so it's a stabbing tree, is it? That does it.
Data.ReadingHint: Feel the grain of the saw's wooden handle in your bare hand. As the saw bites into tree bark, a retaliatory rain of knives comes stabbing down around you. But you don't even flinch. You will saw this stabbing tree down.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: There's nothing more dangerous than a tree that likes to <kanji>stab</kanji>, so you get your <reading>saw</reading> (さ) and start to saw it down. Trees that stab get sawed.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You walk into this <radical>tree</radical> and hit your <radical>head</radical> on it, then a <radical>knife</radical> falls out of it to <kanji>stab</kanji> you. Ow! What the heck?! What was a knife doing in that tree?
